metadata.title: Imposter Syndrome: What the Research Actually Says
metadata.note: CLI video in the REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(slate) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Imposter syndrome was named in 1978 by Clance and Imes, who studied high-achieving women and found a pattern: despite objective evidence of competence, these individuals believed their success was a mistake — luck, timing, or deception — and that they were about to be found out. The phenomenon is now documented across genders, disciplines, and career stages. What is less widely understood is that it produces specific behavioral consequences that harm academic performance in measurable ways — not just a feeling, a set of actions.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Imposter syndrome is not a feeling — it is a behavior pattern.' — Clance and Imes 1978 as anchor; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: One command. Ask Claude to research imposter syndrome in college students — definition, prevalence, two behavioral consequences that harm academic performance, and the most evidence-supported intervention, all under five hundred words, with unverifiable statistics flagged.

props.command: claude "Research imposter syndrome in college students. Provide: (1) definition and prevalence among high-achieving students; (2) two specific behavioral consequences that harm academic performance; (3) most evidence-supported intervention — cite one study. Under 500 words. Flag unverifiable statistics. Use Clance and Imes 1978 as anchor reference."

beats.narration_text: The output is a markdown file — imposter syndrome brief dot md. Three sections: definition with the Clance and Imes anchor, two behavioral consequences named specifically, and one intervention with a study citation. Unverifiable prevalence statistics are flagged in-line. The brief is under five hundred words — short enough to read before an exam.

props.code:
# imposter_syndrome_brief.md — sourced research brief output

## Definition & Prevalence
Clance & Imes (1978): persistent belief that one's success is undeserved
despite objective evidence of competence; fear of being 'found out'.
⚠ Prevalence: '70% of people' figure is widely cited but lacks robust
primary sourcing — treat as directional, not precise.

## Two Behavioral Consequences
1. **Help-avoidance**: Students avoid asking questions or attending
   office hours to prevent exposing perceived incompetence.
   → Direct harm: fewer instructor contacts, lower grade on ambiguous tasks.
2. **Over-preparation + procrastination cycle**: Excessive prep time on
   low-stakes tasks; avoidance of high-stakes tasks until crisis point.
   → Direct harm: uneven performance, exam panic from avoidance.

## Evidence-Supported Intervention
Expressive writing about imposter thoughts (Zhao et al. 2021, J. of
Applied Psychology): reduced performance gap between high-IS and
low-IS students on subsequent problem-solving task. One 20-minute
writing session. Low cost, high accessibility.

beats.narration_text: The brief lands. Three sections, under five hundred words. The two behavioral consequences are named specifically enough to be recognizable: help-avoidance and the over-preparation plus procrastination cycle. Both produce measurable academic harm. The intervention from Zhao and colleagues 2021 is a single twenty-minute expressive writing session.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of the research brief in terminal — two behavioral consequence bullet points visible; Zhao et al. citation and '20-minute' intervention highlighted

beats.narration_text: Now make it personal. Ask Claude to generate the expressive writing prompt from the Zhao 2021 intervention — the actual prompt a student would write to, as a self-administered exercise before a high-stakes exam.

props.command: claude "Generate the expressive writing prompt from the Zhao et al. 2021 imposter syndrome intervention — adapted for a college student about to take a high-stakes exam. The prompt should take 15-20 minutes to complete and follow the structure of the original research protocol."

beats.narration_text: The writing prompt lands. Four questions, structured to move from naming the imposter thought to describing the evidence against it to identifying what you actually know. The protocol is fifteen to twenty minutes, done the night before the exam — not the morning of.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of the four-question expressive writing prompt — exam-specific context, time guidance visible, protocol structure clear

beats.narration_text: Clance and Imes named it in 1978. Zhao and colleagues quantified an intervention in 2021. The forty-three years between those two papers produced a lot of awareness and not much actionable protocol. The expressive writing exercise is the most evidence-supported thing a student can do in twenty minutes the night before a high-stakes exam — and it costs nothing.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Imposter syndrome = behavior, not feeling. Intervention = 20-min writing, night before.' — Clance 1978 to Zhao 2021 as timeline

beats.narration_text: Your move: before your next high-stakes exam, run the writing prompt. Set a timer for twenty minutes. Do not edit while you write — the research protocol is stream-of-consciousness. Then check whether you notice a change in your exam-day state. One data point is not a study, but it is more than you had before.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run writing prompt night before next exam, 2) set 20-min timer — no editing, 3) notice pre-exam state change

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
